title: Open Science & Scholarly Publishing — Self-Assessment Quiz
text:
A static, self-assessment quiz on open science and scholarly publishing, built with Quarto and Observable JS (OJS) and designed to be published on GitHub Pages. The whole question bank lives in one CSV file, so you can add or edit questions without touching any code.

Audience: scholarly communications librarians (and researchers, research infrastructure providers, and technologists in the same space).
No backend: no server, no database, no accounts. Everything runs in the browser; a page refresh resets the session.




.
├── _quarto.yml            # Quarto project + website configuration
├── index.qmd              # The quiz page (landing → questions → summary)
├── about.qmd              # Background, levels, areas, and sourcing notes
├── styles.css             # Styles for the quiz component
├── data/
│   └── questions.csv       # THE QUESTION BANK — single source of truth
├── .github/
│   └── workflows/
│       └── publish.yml     # GitHub Action to build & deploy to GitHub Pages
└── README.md





Install Quarto (v1.4 or newer): https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/. No other toolchain is required — the quiz uses only Quarto’s built-in Observable JS support, so there is no npm install step.
Preview with live reload from the project root:
quarto preview
This opens the site in your browser and reloads on save. Because the quiz reads the CSV at runtime, editing data/questions.csv and refreshing shows your changes immediately.
Render a production build into _site/:
quarto render
The _site/ directory is a self-contained static site you can host anywhere.





Open data/questions.csv in a spreadsheet application or a text editor and add one row per question. Commit and push — that’s the entire workflow.










Column
Required
Description




id
yes
Unique question ID (e.g. PID-012).


level
yes
Exactly one of: Beginner, Intermediate, Expert, Master.


area
yes
The primary topic area. Must match one of the areas below exactly.


question
yes
The question text.


option_a … option_e
yes
The five answer options. Provide all five.


correct_option
yes
The letter of the correct option: a, b, c, d, or e.


explanation_correct
yes
Why the correct answer is correct (shown after answering).


explanation_incorrect
recommended
Why common wrong answers are wrong (shown when the user is wrong).


source_url
optional
Link to authoritative further reading. Leave blank if none.


review_note
optional
Internal note flagging a fact to re-verify. Never shown to users.






The area value must be one of these strings (they drive the topic checkboxes):
Persistent Identifiers
Research Data Management
Open Access
Research Assessment & Metrics
Research Integrity
Reproducibility & Open Methods
Scholarly Infrastructure & Organizations
Preprints & Peer Review
Open Source & Software
Policy & Funder Mandates
If you add a question whose area is not in this list, it is hidden and a warning is logged to the browser console. To introduce a brand-new area, add it to the AREA_ORDER array near the top of the quiz cell in index.qmd.




The file is UTF-8. Any field containing a comma, quote, or line break must be wrapped in double quotes; a double quote inside a quoted field is escaped by doubling it (""). Spreadsheet apps do this automatically on export — the main pitfall is hand-editing.
Vary which option holds the correct answer (don’t always use the same letter).
Aim for exactly five options per question; the UI expects five.




A quick check that every row is well-formed (Python 3, no dependencies):
python - <<'PY'
import csv
req = ["id","level","area","question","option_a","option_b","option_c",
       "option_d","option_e","correct_option","explanation_correct"]
levels = {"Beginner","Intermediate","Expert","Master"}
with open("data/questions.csv", newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    rows = list(csv.DictReader(f))
ids = set()
for i, r in enumerate(rows, 2):  # row 2 = first data row
    for c in req:
        assert r.get(c, "").strip(), f"row {i}: missing {c}"
    assert r["level"] in levels, f"row {i}: bad level {r['level']!r}"
    assert r["correct_option"] in "abcde", f"row {i}: bad correct_option"
    assert r["id"] not in ids, f"row {i}: duplicate id {r['id']}"
    ids.add(r["id"])
print(f"OK: {len(rows)} questions, all valid.")
PY





Two options:


This repo includes .github/workflows/publish.yml, which renders the site and deploys it on every push to main.

Push the repository to GitHub.
In Settings → Pages, set Source to GitHub Actions.
Push to main. The action builds _site/ and deploys it. Your site will be at https://<user>.github.io/<repo>/.
Update the github link in _quarto.yml (website.navbar.tools) to point at your repository.




quarto publish gh-pages
This renders and pushes to a gh-pages branch. Then set Settings → Pages → Source to Deploy from a branch and choose gh-pages.

Project vs. user site paths: the quiz loads the CSV via a relative path, so it works whether the site is served from a subpath (user.github.io/repo/) or a root domain. No base-URL configuration needed.







Why Quarto + OJS. Quarto renders to a plain static site (ideal for GitHub Pages) and keeps content, styling, and logic in one maintainable repo. OJS is used for its strengths: loading the CSV (FileAttachment(...).csv()) and embedding the interactive component.
Why the quiz is one vanilla-JS component, not many reactive OJS cells. A quiz is inherently stateful — current question, running score, locked answers. Observable’s reactive model is built for dataflow (recompute when inputs change), which is awkward for step-by-step mutable state. So the quiz engine is a single self-contained DOM component (built with plain JavaScript and a closure over its state) inside one OJS cell that depends only on the loaded questions. This keeps the state model simple and predictable, and still lives entirely within the Quarto/OJS setup. If you later wanted zero Quarto dependency, this same component would port to a plain index.html + quiz.js + d3.csv with almost no change.
One question at a time. Immediate per-question feedback is the core of a self-assessment learning tool, so questions are shown singly rather than in batches — the explanation lands at the teachable moment.
Accessibility. Native form controls (radios/checkboxes) and buttons for keyboard navigation; visible focus rings; results conveyed by icon and text, never colour alone; aria-live feedback region; contrast chosen for WCAG AA; honours prefers-reduced-motion.
Session length. If more than 15 questions match the chosen filters, a random 15 are asked (constant MAX_QUESTIONS in index.qmd); the user is told how many of the pool are being used.





Questions were written from the referenced organisations’ own documentation and policy pages, and each row records a source_url. Rows with a review_note value flag facts worth re-verifying before wider publication (e.g. figures that grow over time, or terms whose definitions are contested). Corrections are welcome via issues or pull requests to data/questions.csv.



Suggested: content (the CSV) under CC BY 4.0 and code under MIT. Add LICENSE files to confirm before publishing.

title: About this quiz
text: This is a self-assessment quiz on open science and scholarly publishing, aimed primarily at scholarly communications librarians, but useful to researchers, research infrastructure providers, and technologists who work in this space. It is a learning tool, not a test with stakes: nothing is recorded, and you can retake it as often as you like.

title: About this quiz
text: This is a self-assessment quiz on open science and scholarly publishing, aimed primarily at scholarly communications librarians, but useful to researchers, research infrastructure providers, and technologists who work in this space. It is a learning tool, not a test with stakes: nothing is recorded, and you can retake it as often as you like.

title: About this quiz
text:
How it works

Choose one difficulty level and one or more topic areas.
Answer the matching questions one at a time.
After each answer you see whether you were right, an explanation, and (where available) a link to authoritative further reading.
At the end you get a score and, if you picked more than one area, a breakdown by area.

Questions live in a single CSV file in the project repository (data/questions.csv) and are loaded in your browser when the page opens. The site is fully static — there is no server, database, or account.

title: About this quiz
text:
Difficulty levels







Level
Focus




Beginner
Foundational definitions — “what is X”


Intermediate
How things work and how pieces connect


Expert
Nuanced distinctions, standards and policy detail, comparing similar tools


Master
Edge cases, governance and history, cross-system interoperability, and debates in the field

title: About this quiz
text:
Topic areas

Persistent Identifiers (DOI, ORCID, ROR, RRID, IGSN, ARK, …)
Research Data Management (FAIR, CARE, DMPs, repositories)
Open Access (models, licensing, rights retention)
Research Assessment & Metrics (DORA, CoARA, Leiden Manifesto, JIF, h-index)
Research Integrity (misconduct definitions, COPE, retractions)
Reproducibility & Open Methods (preregistration, Registered Reports, TOP)
Scholarly Infrastructure & Organizations (Crossref, DataCite, OpenAlex, POSI, …)
Preprints & Peer Review (arXiv, bioRxiv, open review, overlay journals)
Open Source & Software (software citation, JOSS, Zenodo archiving)
Policy & Funder Mandates (Plan S, the 2022 OSTP memo, UNESCO, NIH DMS)

title: About this quiz
text:
Accuracy and sources
Questions were written using the referenced organisations’ own documentation, FAQs, and policy pages rather than from memory, because terminology, governance, and scope in this area change over time. Each question carries a source_url pointing to the documentation consulted. A small number of questions are flagged internally (in the CSV’s review_note column) where a fact should be re-verified before wider publication — for example, figures that grow over time or terms with contested definitions. If you spot an error, please open an issue or edit data/questions.csv and submit a pull request.

title: About this quiz
text:
Privacy
There is no analytics, no login, and no storage of your answers. Refreshing the page resets everything.

title: Open Science & Scholarly Publishing Self-Assessment
